EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between January 28th and January 29th, a strong low pressure system organized over the Gulf of Alaska, then moved north towards the Prince William Sound.  This low was able to interact favorably with an extremely cold air mass entrenched over the southern Mainland, producing very high snow to liquid water ratios for the duration of the snowfall event across much of Southcentral.||The deep layer of cold air was able to stay mostly in place across the Mat-Su Valleys and Cook Inlet region as moderate to heavy snow moved in near the approaching low.  This lead to the extremely unusual combination of surface temperatures between 0 and 5F and heavy snow that lasted for many hours between 1/28 and 1/29.  This storm had widespread impacts to travel due to the prolonged nature of moderate to heavy snowfall rates for numerous locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT closed the Richardson Highway, including Thompson Pass, around 10:40 am Jan 28, from MP 12-42 due to heavy snow and the potential for avalanche mitigation. Meanwhile, the Valdez Avalanche Center issued an Avalanche Warning for mountains surrounding Valdez and Thompson Pass due to the avalanche danger in place. DOT eventually reported 5 to 10 inches of snow along the Richardson Hwy between MP 12-42. ||Other local storm reports include: 28 inches, 30 inches, and 42 inches of snow in and around Valdez. There was also a snow report of 42 inches near Robe Lake in Valdez.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong offshore winds developed across the Southwest Coast ahead of a strong low pressure moving over the Bering Sea.  Small amounts of falling snow were observed for this event, with the strong winds acting as the main contributor to lowered visibility in blowing snow.  Numerous AWOS/ASOS and mesonet sites were not funtional during the event, but whiteout conditions were observed on several webcams along the Kuskokwim Delta Coast from the evening of 1/2 to the early morning of 1/3.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toksok Bay AWOS recorded 1/4 mile visibility with falling/blowing snow from around 9 PM 1/2 to 1 AM AKST 1/3.  The wind sensor at the observation site was down for the course of the event, but a nearby mesonet site at Chefornak consistently recorded winds around 35 to 45 mph during this timeframe.  Webcam imagery along the coast helped verify conditions on the ground and showed extremely poor visibility along the Kuskokwim Delta Coast from the evening of 1/2 to early morning of 1/3.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over Siberia pushed a very cold Arctic air mass south over mainland Alaska on Jan 26 & 27. Low pressure in the Gulf helped draw colder air southward and kept a modest northerly wind across much of Southwest Alaska, leading to cold wind chills below -40F at several locations across Bristol Bay and the Kuskokwim Delta for multiple days near the end of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous observation sites across the Kuskokwim Delta recorded wind chills persistently between -40 and -50F, mostly between 1/27 and 1/29 with northerly winds between 10 and 20 mph.  The coldest wind chill values were recorded in Eek, where values dipped to -51F during the early morning hours of 1/29.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over Siberia pushed a very cold Arctic air mass south over mainland Alaska on Jan 26 & 27. Low pressure in the Gulf helped draw colder air southward and kept a modest northerly wind across much of Southwest Alaska, leading to cold wind chills below -40F at several locations across Bristol Bay and the Kuskokwim Delta for multiple days near the end of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observation sites across Bristol Bay and the northern Alaska Peninsula recorded periods lasting several hours of northerly winds above 15 mph and wind chills lower than -40F.  Naknek recorded the longest stretch with wind chills below -40F between 10 PM AKST 1/27 and 4 PM AKST 1/28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between January 28th and January 29th, a strong low pressure system organized over the Gulf of Alaska, then moved north towards the Prince William Sound.  This low was able to interact favorably with an extremely cold air mass entrenched over the southern Mainland, producing very high snow to liquid water ratios for the duration of the snowfall event across much of Southcentral.||The deep layer of cold air was able to stay mostly in place across the Mat-Su Valleys and Cook Inlet region as moderate to heavy snow moved in near the approaching low.  This lead to the extremely unusual combination of surface temperatures between 0 and 5F and heavy snow that lasted for many hours between 1/28 and 1/29.  This storm had widespread impacts to travel due to the prolonged nature of moderate to heavy snowfall rates for numerous locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT reported up to 7 inches of snow on the Richardson Hwy between mileposts 42 and 82 at 0845 January 28th. Additionally, travel was severely hampered for the duration of the snow event as areas along the Richardson Hwy, including Thompson Pass, were closed due to heavy snow. Additionally, a Borough Official reported a storm total snow amount of 16 inches at 0800 January 29th in McCarthy, a town affected by the road closures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of deep northerly flow and strong cold air advection brought strong and gusty gap winds to much of Southcentral Alaska between 1/17 and 1/18.  The strongest wind gusts as high as 83 mph occurred through Thompson Pass on the evening of 1/17 at the peak of the northerly wind event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Department of Transportation (RWIS) observation site along the Richardson Highway in Thompson Pass recorded a gust to 83 mph at 9:30 PM AKST 1/17.  Gusts as high as 70 mph were also recorded at multiple sites in the port of Valdez from the evening of 1/17 to very early morning of 1/18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another potent low pressure system moved up into the central Aleutians as it rapidly deepened and reached peak intensity upon entering the far southern Bering Sea.  A core of very strong westerly winds with gusts peaking well into Hurricane force passed over the eastern Aleutians as the low progressed into the Bering, with the highest recorded gust of 102 mph noted at an Akutan marine exchange observation site at 4:45 PM ASKT 1/6/24. Other gusts in excess of 70 mph were recorded from the central Aleutians to the Alaska Peninsula.  ||The low's front continued to cause hazardous weather as it extended across southern mainland Alaska on 1/6 and 1/7. Blizzard conditions were observed in the Kuskokwim Delta and heavy snow fell in Turnagain Pass on the Kenai Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters from the Chugach National Forest Avalanche Information Center (CNFAIC) reported 20 inches of snow fell at road level through Turnagain Pass on Jan 7. The snow line was between 600 and 800 ft, meaning precipitation that fell through the pass was likely all snow. No wind information was available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another potent low pressure system moved up into the central Aleutians as it rapidly deepened and reached peak intensity upon entering the far southern Bering Sea.  A core of very strong westerly winds with gusts peaking well into Hurricane force passed over the eastern Aleutians as the low progressed into the Bering, with the highest recorded gust of 102 mph noted at an Akutan marine exchange observation site at 4:45 PM ASKT 1/6/24. Other gusts in excess of 70 mph were recorded from the central Aleutians to the Alaska Peninsula.  ||The low's front continued to cause hazardous weather as it extended across southern mainland Alaska on 1/6 and 1/7. Blizzard conditions were observed in the Kuskokwim Delta and heavy snow fell in Turnagain Pass on the Kenai Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were observed by the Mekoryuk AWOS beginning on the morning of Jan 6 as a storm force low tracking into the Bering Sea pushed its occluded front across Southwest Alaska. Winds gusted as high as 45 knots (52 mph) while visibility remained one quarter mile or less. FAA webcam images from Toksook Bay showed near whiteout conditions through around 2 pm, though the AWOS at that site was not reporting. However, this suggests that blizzard conditions occurred across much of northwestern portion of the Kuskokwim Delta and Nunivak Island. Further south and east, warmer temperatures and limited snowfall were observed. Visibility improved through the afternoon as the front lifted north and east, bringing precipitation to an end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another potent low pressure system moved up into the central Aleutians as it rapidly deepened and reached peak intensity upon entering the far southern Bering Sea.  A core of very strong westerly winds with gusts peaking well into Hurricane force passed over the eastern Aleutians as the low progressed into the Bering, with the highest recorded gust of 102 mph noted at an Akutan marine exchange observation site at 4:45 PM ASKT 1/6/24. Other gusts in excess of 70 mph were recorded from the central Aleutians to the Alaska Peninsula.  ||The low's front continued to cause hazardous weather as it extended across southern mainland Alaska on 1/6 and 1/7. Blizzard conditions were observed in the Kuskokwim Delta and heavy snow fell in Turnagain Pass on the Kenai Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds were first observed in Dutch Harbor on the morning of Jan 6, with gusts in excess of 65 knots (75 mph). However, the highest gusts were observed in Akutan later in the afternoon. At 4:45 pm, the C-MAN station AKXA2 in Akutan measured a gust of 89 knots (102 mph). Winds then decreased through the evening hours. Additionally, the storm caused some damage, with a boat flooding due to the weight of the rain and snow and a breakwater which had been damaged previously receiving additional damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low pushed a front across the Alaska Peninsula on the evening of January 12. The resulting southeasterly gap flow led to a prolonged period of several hours during which high wind gusts were observed in Cold Bay. The low tracked across the eastern Bering Sea on January 13, pushing into the Kuskokwim Delta that evening. Northwesterly flow brought in cold air behind the low as it continued north, which led to gusty winds and blowing snow on Nunivak Island and along the Kuskokwim Delta coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing snow led to half mile visibility recorded at the Mekoryuk AWOS from 8:15 pm January 13 through 12:50 am January 14. Winds gusted as high as 44 knots (51 mph) during this period. Visibility improved through the morning hours of January 14 as snow intensity decreased with the low exiting into the Yukon Delta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracking across the central Aleutians into the Bering Sea in the early morning hours of January 10 brought blizzard-like conditions and light freezing rain to portions of the Kuskokwim Delta through the evening. Though the low's front continued to bring light snow and rain to Southwest Alaska into January 11, visibility had improved and winds diminished as the low and front weakened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk AWOS recorded several hours of blowing snow with visibility of one half mile or less from 6:55 am through 11:45 am on the morning of January 10. During that period, winds gusted as high as 39 knots (45 mph). Blizzard-like conditions were observed at times. Low visibility conditions were also seen in webcam imagery from Toksook Bay, though the AWOS was not operating. Further inland, winds were lighter and visibility was better, though freezing rain was observed briefly around 5 pm at the Bethel Airport as precipitation transitioned from snow to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low tracked across the Alaska Peninsula into Bristol Bay on the evening of January 11, the low center passing near King Salmon during the early morning hours of January 12. Strong gap winds out of the Western Alaska/Aleutian Range brought high winds to areas around Lake Iliamna on the morning of January 12.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Iliamna ASOS recorded several wind gusts in excess of 65 knots (75 mph) between 2 and 4 am on January 12. The highest wind gust measuring 69 knots (79 mph) was occurred at 3:31 am. Several nearby RAWS and USARRY weather stations also recorded wind gusts at elevation (above 1,000 ft) in excess of 65 knots (75 mph).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low pushed a front across the Alaska Peninsula on the evening of January 12. The resulting southeasterly gap flow led to a prolonged period of several hours during which high wind gusts were observed in Cold Bay. The low tracked across the eastern Bering Sea on January 13, pushing into the Kuskokwim Delta that evening. Northwesterly flow brought in cold air behind the low as it continued north, which led to gusty winds and blowing snow on Nunivak Island and along the Kuskokwim Delta coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cold Bay ASOS recorded several hours during which winds gusted in excess of 65 knots (75 mph) from around 4 pm through 11 pm January 12. Wind gusts measured as strong as 76 knots (88 mph). Wind gusts elsewhere along the Alaska Peninsula were generally measured 40 knots (46 mph) or less during the same time period.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN NORTON SOUND AND NULATO HILLS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High Pressure over the Yukon Territory combined with an area of low pressure in the Bering Sea brought high easterly winds to Unalakleet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed at Unalakleet the morning of the 3rd and continued into the early morning hours of the 4th. Unalakleet reported a peak gust of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong gap winds developed through the Alaska Range on January 7th 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High Winds developed in the early morning hours of the 7th. The Antler Creek mesonet station reported gusts as high as 72 mph from the south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea combined with a strong area of high pressure over the Arctic Ocean created a tight pressure gradient along the Arctic Coast of Alaska. As the pressure gradient tightened, strong winds developed creating blizzard conditions along the Beaufort Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed the morning of the 7th and continued through the evening of the 7th. Alpine reported a peak wind gust of 43 mph during the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea combined with a strong area of high pressure over the Arctic Ocean created a tight pressure gradient along the Arctic Coast of Alaska. As the pressure gradient tightened, strong winds developed creating blizzard conditions along the Beaufort Sea Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed the morning of the 7th and continued through the evening of the 7th, first at Barter Island and then at Point Thomson. Barter Island reported a peak wind gust of 53 mph during the event, while Point Thomson reported a peak wind gust of 56 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea combined with a strong area of high pressure over the Yukon Territory brought high winds to the West Coast of Alaska as well as passes in the Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed early the morning of the 11th and continued through late morning. Kotzebue reported a peak wind gust of 67 mph. The strong winds caused blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea combined with a strong area of high pressure over the Yukon Territory brought high winds to the West Coast of Alaska as well as passes in the Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed the morning of the of the 11th and continued to the evening of the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea combined with a strong area of high pressure over the Yukon Territory brought high winds to the West Coast of Alaska as well as passes in the Alaska Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly gap winds developed the afternoon of the 11th and continued until just after midnight on the 12th. The DOT Antler Creek mesonet station reported a park gust of 78 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the Arctic combined with a strong area of low pressure over southern Alaska created a tight pressure gradient over the eastern Arctic Coast resulting in strong winds and two separate periods of blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Barter Island the afternoon of the 29th and died off by early evening. Barter Island reported a peak wind gust of 51 mph during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure over the Arctic combined with a strong area of low pressure over southern Alaska created a tight pressure gradient over the eastern Arctic Coast resulting in strong winds and two separate periods of blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at both Point Thomson and Barter Island on the morning of the 31st and continued into the evening of the 31st. Barter Island reported a peak wind gust of 48 mph during the event while Point Thomson reported a peak gust of 38 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system slowly tracked over the panhandle, then stalled. An isothermal layer and outflow winds caused snow to continue across Haines and Juneau, while warming led to mixing and compaction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through the day on the 9th, rain periodically mixed with snow, causing accumulations to be wet. An estimated 5 inches was reported in Douglas by late afternoon on social media, then another report estimated 10 to 12 inches in the same area by 550pm. Elsewhere in Juneau, reports of 1 to 4 inches were received. Around 6 inches of snow had fallen by 630pm before changing to rain at 27 mile out the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system slowly tracked over the panhandle, then stalled. An isothermal layer and outflow winds caused snow to continue across Haines and Juneau, while warming led to mixing and compaction.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 2 inches of snow with heavy snowfall rates between noon and 3pm in downtown Haines. Another spotter reported on social media that 6.5 inches had fallen by 5pm on the Chilkat Peninsula, then had 9 inches by later in the night. Another trained spotted reported that between 8am and 8pm, snowfall had reached a total of 7 inches, with snow having stopped. A report was also received of freezing rain during the morning time frame in Haines with some ice accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds in Skagway were enhanced, reaching high wind criteria, as a front developed as it crossed overhead. Some accumulating and drifting snow also occurred near White Pass along the Klondike Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing and drifting snow was observed on the Klondike Highway RWIS webcams between 11am and 3pm. By the morning report the next day, they estimated 2 to 6 inches had fallen with up to 1 foot drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent southerly winds in Skagway were enhanced, reaching high wind criteria, as a front developed as it crossed overhead. Some accumulating and drifting snow also occurred near White Pass along the Klondike Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high wind event at Skagway saw southerly wind gusts in excess of 60 mph at the boat harbor and the airport throughout the day on the 22nd. A peak gust of 68 mph was measured at 136pm at the airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A past NWS Employee estimated 6 to 8 inches of dry fluffy snow by 815am on the 27th. The WSO confirmed 7.1 inches by 845am with only a trace of additional accumulation falling through the rest of the day. The Yakutat airport was reporting gusts of 20-25 mph and visibility down to a half mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Employee estimated 6 inches of snow by 222pm on the 27th with variable coverage due to blowing and drifting. By the following morning there was a range of 8.6 inches total measured in town and 12.6 inches at the WSO. The Metlakatla Seaplane Base measured gusts of 20 to 30 mph with visibility down to a quarter mile briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter south of Wrangell measured 4 inches of snow by 8am on the 27th with 1 mile visibility. By 7pm their total was up to 6.5 inches. Intermittent moderate snowfall continued through the night with another 2 inches falling, but the total compressing down to 7.25 inches. The Wrangell airport observer measured periodic gusts of 20-30 mph during the day on the 27th and visibility down to a quarter mile briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sitka Airport measured gusts of 30-50mph through the morning of the 27th, then continued gusts of 20-30mph through the rest of the day a visibility briefly down to a quarter mile at times. Trained spotters reported variable snowfall amounts due to blowing and drifting snow in the morning of the 27th. Through the morning of the 28th, an estimated 8.25 inches of snow had fallen.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pelican COOP measured 2.4 inches of snow by 8am on the 27th and another 8.1 inches by the following morning. Based on other communities nearby, greatest snowfall rates were likely during the day on the 27th.

CZ_NAME: WESTERN KUPREANOF ISLAND AND KUIU ISLAND
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Security Bay, SW of Kake, reported 9.5 inches of snow already by 11am on the 27th along with blowing and drifting snow. The spotter reported another 6 inches by 11pm and another 3 inches the following morning for a total of 18.5 inches. A spotter in Kake reported 8-12 inches of snow by 8am on the 28th with significant drifting. The Kake airport was reporting wind gusts of around 20 mph and visibility down to three quarters of a mile at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported blowing and drifting snow through the day on the 27th with about 4 inches of snow by late afternoon. By the following morning another 6 inches of snow had fallen for storm totals ranging from 8.2 inches at the north end of Ketchikan to 12 inches in town. The Ketchikan Airport measured gusts of 20 to 40 mph with visibility down to a quarter mile briefly.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Gustavus measured 4.5 inches of snow by 9am on the 27th and up to 6 inches by 2pm. Intermittent moderate snowfall continued through the night with another 2 inches falling, but the total compressing down to 7.5 inches. The nearby COOP measured 6 inches from this event. The Gustavus airport AWOS was down at the time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public comment on social media reported 7 inches already in Hoonah by 7am on the 27th. The Hoonah COOP station at the water treatment plan measured 12 inches of snow by 9am along with very poor visibility. The COOP measured another 4.8 inches by the following morning. Other social media reports in Hoonah noted an estimated 18 inches in under 6 hours and another shared a photo of 2 feet as of 1235pm with snow continuing to fall heavily. The Hoonah airport was reporting gusts of 20-30 mph and visibility down to a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving system with cold air in place brought widespread snowfall across the panhandle. Blowing snow with reduced visibility and heavy rates were observed followed by post-frontal snow showers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public comments on social media from around Prince of Wales Island noted highly variable snowfall amounts due to blowing and drifting snow. The first report was for up to 12 inches in Coffman Cove by 126pm on the 27th. Other reports ranged from 14 inches in Thorne Bay, confirmed by the COOP, to over 6 inches in Whale Pass through the following morning. Locations on the western side of the Island saw lower accumulations with exception of Naukati Bay which saw about 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force Bering Sea low pushed a front up into the Southwest Alaska coast between 2/4 and 2/5.  Temperatures remained cold enough for precipitation to fall as snow, and strong southeasterly winds caused areas of significant falling and blowing snow along portions of the coast as the frontal system moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toksook Bay AWOS recorded visibility predominantly at a half mile with 25 to 35 mph sustained winds in snow and blowing snow between 7:45 AM and 11 AM AKST 2/5.  Similar conditions were likely occurring along other portions of the Kuskokwim Delta Coast, but observations were not available.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm force Bering Sea low pushed a front up into the Southwest Alaska coast between 2/4 and 2/5.  Temperatures remained cold enough for precipitation to fall as snow, and strong southeasterly winds caused areas of significant falling and blowing snow along portions of the coast as the frontal system moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Koliganek AWOS recorded visibility down to 1/4 to 1/2 mile with 30 to 37 mph sustained winds in snow and blowing snow between 5:30 AM and 8:30 AM AKST 2/5.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure in place over Northern Canada and extending into mainland Alaska worked in tandem with a deep upper trough over the Bering Sea to draw moisture north along an atmospheric river that remained in place across the Alaska Peninsula and into Southwest Alaska from February 14th through February 16th. A series of shortwave troughs driving northward led to several rounds of heavy precipitation for the area. There were two main shortwaves - the first brought snow to the south side of the Alaska Peninsula beginning early on Feb 14, pushing north into Bristol Bay and the Kuskokwim Delta, and westward along the Alaska Peninsula through the day. The second arrived during the afternoon/evening of Feb 15, bringing warmer air and reinforced heavier precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toksook Bay AWOS recorded 1/4 to 1/2 mile visibility in blowing snow with 20 to 27 mph sustained winds between 10 AM and 3 PM AKST 2/13.  Low visibility was also showing up on area webcams along the rest of the Kuskokwim Delta Coast during this time, including near Kipnuk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure in place over Northern Canada and extending into mainland Alaska worked in tandem with a deep upper trough over the Bering Sea to draw moisture north along an atmospheric river that remained in place across the Alaska Peninsula and into Southwest Alaska from February 14th through February 16th. A series of shortwave troughs driving northward led to several rounds of heavy precipitation for the area. There were two main shortwaves - the first brought snow to the south side of the Alaska Peninsula beginning early on Feb 14, pushing north into Bristol Bay and the Kuskokwim Delta, and westward along the Alaska Peninsula through the day. The second arrived during the afternoon/evening of Feb 15, bringing warmer air and reinforced heavier precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Anchorage called out multiple times to the Chignik city office to get an estimate of total snowfall they received.  They reported a 9 inch storm total snowfall between February 13th and 2:30 PM AKST February 14th.  Upon follow up on the morning of February 15th, the office estimated about a foot of new snowfall had fallen the previous day, in addition to about another foot overnight through 11 AM AKST February 15th.  Area webcams also showed persistent falling snow and reduced visibility for much of this time period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure in place over Northern Canada and extending into mainland Alaska worked in tandem with a deep upper trough over the Bering Sea to draw moisture north along an atmospheric river that remained in place across the Alaska Peninsula and into Southwest Alaska from February 14th through February 16th. A series of shortwave troughs driving northward led to several rounds of heavy precipitation for the area. There were two main shortwaves - the first brought snow to the south side of the Alaska Peninsula beginning early on Feb 14, pushing north into Bristol Bay and the Kuskokwim Delta, and westward along the Alaska Peninsula through the day. The second arrived during the afternoon/evening of Feb 15, bringing warmer air and reinforced heavier precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Anchorage called out to several communities across Bristol Bay after and during the heavy snowfall event.  The Togiak city office estimated up to 2 feet of snow fell in a little over a day between 12 PM 2/14 and 1 PM 2/15, resulting in a hour long delay in starting school the morning of 2/15.  The Dillingham police department estimated a total of 4 to 8 inches had fallen ending at 3 PM AKST on 2/14, and the office was advising their employees to go home early due to the heavy snow ongoing at the time.  The Port Heiden city office estimated about 6 to 7 inches of new snowfall since yesterday at 1 PM 2/15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure in place over Northern Canada and extending into mainland Alaska worked in tandem with a deep upper trough over the Bering Sea to draw moisture north along an atmospheric river that remained in place across the Alaska Peninsula and into Southwest Alaska from February 14th through February 16th. A series of shortwave troughs driving northward led to several rounds of heavy precipitation for the area. There were two main shortwaves - the first brought snow to the south side of the Alaska Peninsula beginning early on Feb 14, pushing north into Bristol Bay and the Kuskokwim Delta, and westward along the Alaska Peninsula through the day. The second arrived during the afternoon/evening of Feb 15, bringing warmer air and reinforced heavier precipitation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nelson Lagoon AWOS recorded visibility 1/2 mile or less with 25 to 30 mph sustained winds in snow/blowing snow from 3:30 PM to 6:30 PM AKST February 14th.  A radio station in Sand Point also reported 3 inches of new snowfall through 1 PM February 15th and significant drifting of snow due to strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moving into the Southwest Alaska coast produced areas of snow and blowing snow as it moved across the Kuskokwim Delta and Bristol Bay on February 21st.  The worst conditions were focused over Nunivak Island, where several hours of blizzard conditions were observed on the early morning of 2/21.  Conditions nearly as severe were observed with gusty southeast winds ahead of the front along with falling and blowing snow across Bristol Bay, mainly out in Koliganek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk AWOS recorded over 3 hours of winds gusting between 35 and 40 mph and 1/4 mile visibility in snow/blowing snow between 9:30 AM and 1:20 PM AKST 2/21.  Low visibility down to 1/2 mile also pushed inland to Bethel for a short time as the frontal system producing the more severe conditions along the coast moved inland later in the day on 2/21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moving into the Southwest Alaska coast produced areas of snow and blowing snow as it moved across the Kuskokwim Delta and Bristol Bay on February 21st.  The worst conditions were focused over Nunivak Island, where several hours of blizzard conditions were observed on the early morning of 2/21.  Conditions nearly as severe were observed with gusty southeast winds ahead of the front along with falling and blowing snow across Bristol Bay, mainly out in Koliganek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Koliganek AWOS recorded 1/2 mile or lower visibility with 25 to 30 mph winds in snow and blowing snow between 9:30 AM and 1:20 PM AKST 2/21.  Temperatures warmed to or above 32 degrees Fahrenheit later in the afternoon, allowing blowing snow to end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving ahead of a low entering the southern Bering Sea approached the Pribilof Islands from the south on March 18. A cold air mass in place ahead of the front coupled with the sea ice edge only a few miles from the islands allowed for a short period of blizzard conditions to develop as strong east-northeasterly winds and snow moved in. The front reached the Southwest Alaska coast later that afternoon, leading to blizzard conditions along the Kuskokwim Delta coast through the early morning hours of March 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk AWOS observed blizzard conditions with one quarter mile visibility or less with winds 30 knots (35 mph) or greater from around 4 pm on March 18 until just after midnight on March 19. Temperatures began to warm into the mid 30s Fahrenheit as snow turned to rain on the morning of March 19, leading to improved conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving ahead of a low entering the southern Bering Sea approached the Pribilof Islands from the south on March 18. A cold air mass in place ahead of the front coupled with the sea ice edge only a few miles from the islands allowed for a short period of blizzard conditions to develop as strong east-northeasterly winds and snow moved in. The front reached the Southwest Alaska coast later that afternoon, leading to blizzard conditions along the Kuskokwim Delta coast through the early morning hours of March 19.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint Paul ASOS did not report visibility during this event. Winds gusted 35 knots (40 mph) or greater with whiteout conditions seen on FAA webcam imagery between 10 am and 2 pm on March 18, during which temperatures were also below freezing. At 6 pm, an NWS employee at the Saint Paul Weather Service Office measured between 4 to 6 inches of snow from the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very cold Arctic airmass was in place over northern Alaska.  Very cold temperatures were accompanied by brisk east to northeast winds. The combination of the cold temperatures and brisk winds resulted in very cold wind chills near -60 F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very cold wind chills develop during the evening hours of March 10th at the E22K Anaktuvuk Pass USARRAY site.  East to northeast winds of 15 mph were reported accompanied by very cold temperatures to produce wind chills as low as -62 F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Quick on the heels of the previous system, another front pushed onto the Kuskokwim Delta coast on March 19. Despite temperatures near freezing and an east-southeasterly wind, blizzard conditions were observed on Nunivak Island, which was surrounded entirely by sea ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mekoryuk AWOS observed blizzard conditions for several hours through the afternoon and evening of March 19. Winds gusted as high as 55 knots (63 mph) out of the east-southeast as blowing snow reduced visibility to one quarter mile or less. Conditions improved around 8 pm AKDT as temperatures began to warm and rain began to mix in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low tracking northward through the eastern Bering Sea pushed a front across the Pribilof Islands, leading to blizzard conditions in Saint Paul on the evening of March 28. Blizzard conditions were short-lived, lasting only 3 hours, and came to an end late on March 28 as precipitation quickly shifted north of the islands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Saint Paul ASOS recorded a 3 hour period of blizzard conditions on the evening of March 28. Winds gusted as high as 38 knots (44 mph) while blowing snow led to one quarter mile visibility. Conditions improved quickly after 10 pm AKDT as precipitation came to an end.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low tracked into the western Gulf of Alaska and across Kodiak Island on March 30. Its occluded front swept across the Alaska Peninsula through the day as the low pressure drew cold air over the Bering Sea across the peninsula into the Gulf of Alaska. This led to blizzard conditions along portions of the Alaska Peninsula through the afternoon and contributed to high winds through Shelikof Strait affecting the western side of Kodiak Island during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Port Heiden AWOS reported just over three hours of blizzard conditions on the afternoon of March 30. Visibility remained at or below one quarter mile while winds gusted as high as 39 knots (45 mph). Blizzard-like conditions were observed along the Bering Sea coast of the Alaska Peninsula as far north as Naknek, though this occurred for a shorter time period. The Pilot Point AWOS stopped reporting shortly after the onset of blizzard-like conditions, so it is unknown for how long the conditions lasted there.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure over the Arctic and North Slope tightened the pressure gradient along the coast. The combination of strong east winds and falling snow resulted in blizzard conditions developing at Point Lay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon hours of March 4th at Point Lay (PPIZ). Strong east winds with gusts to 48 mph were reported accompanied by visibility in blowing snow of less than a quarter of a mile. Conditions improved late March 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong high pressure built south across the Arctic Coast from a 1048 mb high 400 NM north of the Arctic Coast. This high pressure tightened the pressure gradient along the coast resulting in strong east to northeast winds developing, in particular along the western Arctic coast. Widespread areas of blowing snow accompanied the winds with blizzard conditions developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed after midnight on March 20th at Cape Lisburne (PALU). Strong east to northeast winds with gusts to 71 mph were reported, accompanied by visibility in blowing snow of less than a quarter of mile. Conditions improved during the afternoon hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A North Pacific low tracked into the western Gulf of Alaska and across Kodiak Island on March 30. Its occluded front swept across the Alaska Peninsula through the day as the low pressure drew cold air over the Bering Sea across the peninsula into the Gulf of Alaska. This led to blizzard conditions along portions of the Alaska Peninsula through the afternoon and contributed to high winds through Shelikof Strait affecting the western side of Kodiak Island during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NOAA Ship Oscar Dyson measured a 65 knot (75 mph) wind gust at 58.0N 153.5W, near Cape Uganik at 8 pm AKDT on March 30. Strong southwesterly winds combined with snowfall also brought over an hour of blizzard-like conditions to Akhiok.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front associated with a strong low near the Pribilof Island in conjunction with high pressure over the Chukotsk Peninsula and the Chukchi Sea produced a tight pressure gradient through the Bering Strait. As the front lifted north, strong winds developed across the Bering Strait Coast and St Lawrence Island. The combination of the strong winds and falling snow produced blizzard conditions St Lawrence Island to the Bering Strait.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the morning hours on March 24th at Brevig Mission (PFKT) and Tin City (PATC).  Strong northeast winds with gusts to 60 mph were reported, accompanied by heavy snow. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the late evening at Brevig Mission and during the afternoon hours of March 25th at Tin City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front associated with a strong low near the Pribilof Island in conjunction with high pressure over the Chukotsk Peninsula and the Chukchi Sea produced a tight pressure gradient through the Bering Strait. As the front lifted north, strong winds developed across the Bering Strait Coast and St Lawrence Island. The combination of the strong winds and falling snow produced blizzard conditions St Lawrence Island to the Bering Strait.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon hours on March 23rd at Chevak (PAVA). Northeast winds with gusts to 41 mph were reported, accompanied by heavy snow, resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved after noon on March 24th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of weather fronts moving into SE AK opened the path for a stream of moisture to reach into the northern half of the panhandle. Drainage flow kept temperatures |sufficiently cold enough to support continued snow accumulation in Haines before a shift in wind direction and a change to rain at sea level.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP station at Haines Customs measured 6.5 inches of snow at 7am on the 8th with most of that falling overnight and moderate snow continuing to fall. The station measured an additional 4 inches of snow by the next morning, but with most of that ending by mid day on the 8th, resulting in approximately 10 inches of snow in 12 hours. Snowfall totals in town were 3 to 5 inches followed by a change over to rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low moved north of the Western Arctic Coast dragging a front north of the Brooks Range to the North Slope. As the front lifted north, strong easterly winds developed along the Arctic Coast. The strongest winds occurred over the Eastern Beaufort Sea coast resulting in blizzard conditions at Point Thomson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the late evening hours on March 26th at Point Thomson (PAAD).  East winds with gusts to 48 mph were reported, resulting in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the early morning hours of March 28th.

CZ_NAME: EASTERN ALASKA RANGE NORTH OF TRIMS CAMP
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved from the North Pacific to Bristol Bay to the Interior and the North Slope. This low tightened the pressure gradient across the Alaska Range resulting in high winds in the Eastern Alaska Range and blizzard conditions along the West Coast. Blizzard conditions at Kotzebue continued into the morning hours of April 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly chinook winds developed in the Eastern Alaska Range on March 30th. South winds gusts to 74 mph were reported at Air Force Tower (AFTA2). Winds diminished during the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved from the North Pacific to Bristol Bay to the Interior and the North Slope. This low tightened the pressure gradient across the Alaska Range resulting in high winds in the Eastern Alaska Range and blizzard conditions along the West Coast. Blizzard conditions at Kotzebue continued into the morning hours of April 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the morning hours on March 24th at Brevig Mission (PFKT) and Tin City (PATC).  Strong northeast winds with gusts to 60 mph were reported, accompanied by heavy snow. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the late evening at Brevig Mission and during the afternoon hours of March 25th at Tin City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved from the North Pacific to Bristol Bay to the Interior and the North Slope. This low tightened the pressure gradient across the Alaska Range resulting in high winds in the Eastern Alaska Range and blizzard conditions along the West Coast. Blizzard conditions at Kotzebue continued into the morning hours of April 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the late morning hours on March 31st at Point Hope (PAPO).  Wind gusts to 47 mph were reported, accompanied by falling now. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the evening hours of March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak to moderate strength atmospheric river impacted the panhandle, but temperatures remained cool enough at elevation to enable accumulating snow above sea level. Snow melt cooling quickly brought temperatures down enough for accumulating snow to reach sea level in Haines and Skagway, before stronger southerly winds saw snow change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A burst of heavy wet snow along the Chilkat Peninsula resulted in around 5 inches of snow between 5pm and 11pm on the 13th, then an additional 9 inches by 245am on the 14th as it changed to rain. On the north side of Haines, a measurement of 6.7 inches of snow was reported to have fallen between 9pm and 1250am with snow continuing to fall and a power outage. Haines Customs COOP measured 6 inches of snow by 7am on the 14th and snow had stopped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak to moderate strength atmospheric river impacted the panhandle, but temperatures remained cool enough at elevation to enable accumulating snow above sea level. Snow melt cooling quickly brought temperatures down enough for accumulating snow to reach sea level in Haines and Skagway, before stronger southerly winds saw snow change to rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: DOT closed Klondike Highway due to poor conditions expected to get worse at 548pm on the 13th. The road remained closed due to avalanches on both sides of the border through the following day. Approximately 8 inches of snow was reported via SNOTEL sites at elevation and around 5 inches of snow was reported in town. The transition from snow back to rain was accompanied by a short period of freezing rain in Skagway which was followed by a surge of warm southerly wind and a peak wind gust of 61 mph at the Skagway Airport at 546 AM AKDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving across the panhandle and southern Yukon encouraged the development of a localized lee-side trough across Lynn Canal, leading to high winds for the Klondike Highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 63 mph was recorded at the White Pass RWIS during the morning of March 31st. Wind gusts as high as 58 mph were recorded at the Skagway Airport ASOS. A large tree was reported by law enforcement as down along Dyea Road, resulting in the road being blocked.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved from the North Pacific to Bristol Bay to the Interior and the North Slope. This low tightened the pressure gradient across the Alaska Range resulting in high winds in the Eastern Alaska Range and blizzard conditions along the West Coast. Blizzard conditions at Kotzebue continued into the morning hours of April 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the early morning hours on March 31st at Buckland (PABL).  Wind gusts to 59 mph were reported, accompanied by falling snow. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the evening hours of March 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low moved from the North Pacific to Bristol Bay to the Interior and the North Slope. This low tightened the pressure gradient across the Alaska Range resulting in high winds in the Eastern Alaska Range and blizzard conditions along the West Coast. Blizzard conditions at Kotzebue continued into the morning hours of April 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the afternoon hours on March 31st at Kotzebue (PAOT).  Wind gusts to 50 mph were reported, accompanied by falling snow. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the morning hours of April 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry snow fell across the panhandle as a frontal band moved through. NW drainage flow caused the snow to stack up in Haines over a 36 hour period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter along the Chilkat Peninsula reported 10 inches of light and fluffy snow fell between 10pm on the 5th and 1145am on the 6th. Snow continued through the next morning with another 10 inches reported by the same spotter by 10am on the 7th. During the same period, the Haines COOP in town measured 15 inches, while the Customs station measured 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front associated with a low in the Aleutians moved north across the West Coast. As the front pushed across the region, strong winds developed accompanied by heavy snow.  Blizzard conditions developed along the Yukon Delta Coast, St Lawrence Island, and the Bering Strait Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the evening hours on March 18th at Chevak (PAVA). Strong winds with gusts to 51 mph were reported accompanied by heavy snow, resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved after midnight on March 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front associated with a low in the Aleutians moved north across the West Coast. As the front pushed across the region, strong winds developed accompanied by heavy snow.  Blizzard conditions developed along the Yukon Delta Coast, St Lawrence Island, and the Bering Strait Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed during the early evening hours on March 19th at Savoonga (PASA). Strong winds with gusts to 58 mph were reported, accompanied by heavy snow. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved during the late evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river moving through the Aleutians on March 19th - March 21nd dropped heavy rain of 3-5 inches storm total over the Onalaska/Dutch Harbor area. The heavy rain came in 2 strong pulses, one on 3/19 of up to 3 which started flooding in the area, with a  second pulse overnight on 3/21 which exacerbated flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unalaska Roads Department reported heavy rain in Unalaska washed out several gravel roadways in the surrounding area.  Blocked culverts caused additional street flooding and also washed large crevasses up to 4 ft deep in some of the roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong warm front associated with a low in the Aleutians moved north across the West Coast. As the front pushed across the region, strong winds developed accompanied by heavy snow.  Blizzard conditions developed along the Yukon Delta Coast, St Lawrence Island, and the Bering Strait Coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed overnight on March 19th at Wales (PAIW). Strong winds with gusts to 74 mph were reported, accompanied by heavy snow. This resulted in blizzard conditions developing. Conditions improved after midnight on March 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm force low pressure system over the North Pacific lifted north and moved into the Gulf of Alaska by the morning of January 29th. There was an associated atmospheric river along the warm side of the cold front that had a significant amount of sub-tropical moisture within it. Along with the the high anomalous moisture values, there was a very warm airmass with the atmospheric river which increased freezing levels across Southeast Alaska to more than 5000 feet. There was a deep snowpack at upper elevations and near sea level, which melted with the heavy rain to increase the amount of runoff into rivers and streams. Another low pressure system developed along the frontal boundary which stalled the front over the region to produce continued prolog heavy rain but also high winds. The combination of high winds and heavy precipitation on snow at high elevations triggered a number of avalanches near Haines.  ||The 2 day precipitation total across the northern half of the Southeast Alaska ranged from 2 to over 4 inches along with significant amount of snowmelt and runoff from the high freezing levels and the heavy rain melting the snowpack. The heavy rain triggered a small landslide in Haines near Lutak, another near mile 6 of Zimovia Highway in Wrangell. There was some minor overland flooding from poor drainage near the Haines airport by the afternoon of January 29th. Near the Juneau area there was a lot of minor flooding of small streams going over their banks from the rain and snow runoff through January 30th but there was moderate flooding near Jordan Creek that impacted a few homes, business and roadways.||Tree fall was was widespread across the region from this event due to the saturated ground and high wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread high winds were measured across Marine Exchange mesonet stations throughout the Ketchikan area and at the Ketchikan ASOS. The peak gust measured at Ward Cove reached 83 mph at 343am on the 29th. A COOP at the northern end of Ketchikan reported that at least 80mph winds blew off the top third of a large cedar tree.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter in Pelican reported on social media that there was flying debris and trees down, along with roofing coming loose in places and windows blown out. Later a COOP observer in Pelican reported 67 mph wind gusts were measured at his personal weather station and reported that a roof was coming off of a building and windows had been blown out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low moving northward brought a surge of high winds and upper elevation snow through Skagway as advancing warm air moved up from the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A system advancing northward brought with it a surge of high winds through the morning hours of January 27. The Skagway airport measured a peak gust of 63 mph at 412am and a peak gust of 60 mph was measured at the boat harbor at 1153am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing low moving northward brought a surge of high winds and upper elevation snow through Skagway as advancing warm air moved up from the south.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Klondike Highway was closed due to avalanches reported on the Canadian side of the border at 5am on the 27th, then at 830am one was reported on the US side along with another 4 on the Canadian side. At 904am DOT Klondike report noted at least 8 inches of snow had fallen near the border despite some mixing at the time. Winds of 25 to 50 mph were also noted on the report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain and rising freezing levels moved over the Juneau area through the evening hours of January 28th. The rain continued to increase in intensity and with the rising freezing level, a significant amount of runoff was going into local rivers and streams. Jordan Creek started to rise significantly in the early morning of January 29th and went over minor flood stage of 9.7 feet in the late morning. As the second low pressure system moved over the area rain rates increased again over the area, to be greater than 0.25 inches per hour at times. The amount of runoff from the overland flow into Jordan Creek rose water levels to go over the moderate flood stage of 10.5 feet at 415pm and the creek crested at 10.78 feet in the early evening of January 29th. Rain rates decreased as the cold front moved east of the area in the late evening hours and stream levels on Jordan Creek started to recede through the overnight hours. The creek was below minor flood stage by mid morning of January 30th. There was some damage to a few homes and business along the creek and roadways near Jordan Creek were impassable at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly outflow winds resulted in the development of a mountain wave over Downtown Juneau and Douglas. Outflow winds in the northern panhandle reached down to surface level, resulting in high winds for the town of Skagway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly outflow winds in Skagway resulted in a high wind event with intermittent lulls in the strongest of winds measured at the airport ASOS on the 19th and 20th. The peak wind gust at the ASOS was 66 mph at 1212am on the 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A N-S oriented stationary front set up over the central panhandle Wednesday January 31st with a tight temperature gradient between cold air to the west and very warm air over the southern inner channels. Snow fell along the front in a narrow band centered over Juneau, Angoon, and Kuiu Island. The front slowly shifted eastward through the following day, February 1st, with rain transitioning to snow over Petersburg and northern Prince of Wales Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began to fall in Juneau in the afternoon of January 31st and reaching 7.2 inches measured at the forecast office and 6.8 inches measured at the airport by midnight. Snow continued to fall until 1pm on the 1st with another 7.7 inches measured for the day at the NWS office and 7 inches at the Juneau airport. This gave a total of around 14 inches of snow in less than 24hrs for this event that spanned January and February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A N-S oriented stationary front set up over the central panhandle Wednesday January 31st with a tight temperature gradient between cold air to the west and very warm air over the southern inner channels. Snow fell along the front in a narrow band centered over Juneau, Angoon, and Kuiu Island. The front slowly shifted eastward through the following day, February 1st, with rain transitioning to snow over Petersburg and northern Prince of Wales Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Angoon Community Association (tribe) measured 13 inches of snow that fell from mid-day on the 31st to just before lunchtime on February 1st. An estimated 6 inches fell by midnight on the 31st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over an extended long weekend event, a prefrontal trough followed by a stronger main system with attached moisture plume, delivered first a wave of dry powdery snow with very high snow rations (40:1 to 50:1 in many places). The next wave/front of snow was wetter and difficult to shovel, with some places seeing a period of freezing drizzle in between the two waves. As the 2nd front tracked southward on the 16th, bringing snow to those areas and extending the duration of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pelican COOP Observer measured 13.3 inches of snow had fallen by 8am on the 13th, another 14.5 inches by 8am on the 14th and another 4.7 inches on the 15th when it had changed to rain. A few more inches of additional snow fell on the 16th brining nearly 3 feet of snow to the community over the MLK long weekend event.

The next wave/front of snow was wetter and difficult to shovel, with some places seeing a period of freezing drizzle in between the two waves. As the 2nd front tracked southward on the 16th, bringing snow to those areas and extending the duration of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began in the evening of the 12th with rates picking up after 930pm. The Gustavus DOT station foreman reported 6 inches of dry snow by 527am on the 13th and another trained spotter measured 6.5 inches by 951am. Snow became lighter through the 13th and didn't add up much more due to compression over the next 2 days. A short period of heavy wet snow was noted on the 15th when 2 inches fell before changing to rain. Over the MLK long weekend (3.5 days), a total of 7-11 inches fell across Gustavus, but most of that occurred by Saturday morning the 13th, as noted above.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over an extended long weekend event, a prefrontal trough followed by a stronger main system with attached moisture plume, delivered first a wave of dry powdery snow with very high snow rations (40:1 to 50:1 in many places). The next wave/front of snow was wetter and difficult to shovel, with some places seeing a period of freezing drizzle in between the two waves. As the 2nd front tracked southward on the 16th, bringing snow to those areas and extending the duration of the event.

Snow became lighter through the 13th and didn't add up much more due to compression over the next 2 days. Over the MLK long weekend (3.5 days), a total of 12.9 inches fell in Hoonah, but most of that occurred by Saturday morning the 13th, as noted above.

As the 2nd front tracked southward on the 16th, bringing snow to those areas and extending the duration of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light and very dry snow began to fall the evening of Friday January 12th. After 9pm, rates increased and by 8am the Juneau Waste Water Treatment Plant COOP measured 7 inches of snow and similar amounts were measured at the NWS Weather Forecast Office in the Mendenhall Valley and near Lena Point that morning as well. Snow continued to fall through Saturday with several more inches accumulating then changed to freezing drizzle Sunday morning. This compacted the snow from the day before and created an icy crust. By late Sunday morning precipitation changed back to snow and began to add up again. Snowfall rates were very heavy throughout the Monday holiday and density of the the snow was wet and difficult to move. Storm total snowfall at the NWS Office was 33.1 inches and an NWS employee on North Douglas measured a storm total of 38 inches. ||An avalanche occurred around 6pm Monday along Thane Road in a known avalanche area and another avalanche occurred the following day in the Berhends slide area. No damage occurred from either avalanche. Snow diminished late on the 15th, but schools were closed for Tuesday to allow for continued snow removal. USCG and Juneau Docks & Harbors department confirmed that 3 vessels sank in the harbors due to the weight of the snow with a rough estimated loss of $100k. In all they had 8 vessels that were at is of sinking and a gangway at the Douglas Harbor, they were able to save 5 vessels and the gangway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving up from the SW initiated an overrunning event for the northern panhandle. Favorable NW winds around Haines helped keep precipitation as snow across the Borough while most of the remaining panhandle saw rain. The heaviest snowfall rates were Wednesday night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began around 9am on the 3rd and a trained spotter south of Haines measured 6 inches shortly before 9pm. The Alaska Department of Transportation estimated 8 to 12 inches of snow along parts of the Haines Highway during the early morning hours of the 4th and the Haines Customs COOP measured 10.5 inches by 7am but was still snowing at the time. By 9 AM, trained spotters and COOP observer measured a range of 10 to 15 inches of heavy wet snow near Haines. Snow changed to rain shortly after with a short period of freezing rain.

Light snow had fallen throughout the MLK long week with periods of light freezing rain reported by the ASOS and by the COOP on both the 14th and 15th. The COOP measured a storm total of 13.7 inches over the long duration event while the public reported on social media a storm total of 18.5 inches of snow fell on the Chilkat Peninsula south of Haines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds picked up the blizzard conditions developed at Deadhorse the morning of the 13th and continued through the evening of the 13th. During this event, the winds gusted as high as 66 mph.

The nearby Ketchikan ASOS confirmed that the greatest snowfall rates occurred over a 12 hour period between 2pm and 2am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 984 mb area of low pressure moved north through the Bering Sea on January 16th, and into the southern Chukchi Sea on the 17th. This weather system brought high winds, snow and blowing snow to the west coast of Alaska. After the low moved into the Chuckhi Sea, a strong area of high pressure built in behind it allowing strong winds to continue over northwest Alaska.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds developed on the morning of the 15th and continued into the afternoon. The Hoodoo Hill RAWS reported gusts as high as 63 mph during the event. The high winds also caused blowing snow; however, the visibility did not drop below a mile at any station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly outflow winds resulted in the development of a mountain wave over Downtown Juneau and Douglas. Outflow winds in the northern panhandle reached down to surface level, resulting in high winds for the town of Skagway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long duration Taku wind event, with occasional lulls, was initiated by the formation of a mountain wave over Downtown Juneau and Douglas due to northerly outflow winds. All of the downtown Juneau docks and the Douglas Boat Harbor measured gusts in excess of 60 mph during this period with peaks of over 70 mph and a max of 81 mph at 1252am on the 18th at the AML dock. The weather station at the top of Eaglecrest measured a peak gust of 73 mph at 903pm on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low slowly moving north through the Gulf brought an extended duration snow event to much of the panhandle. Snowfall rates increased across the central panhandle Sunday morning, causing the onset time of the event to begin earlier and resulting in a longer duration snowfall, but warning level rates were confined to the Icy Strait region with greatest amounts in the Juneau area. Dry snow became wetter through the event with southern areas transitioning to rain before seeing significant accumulations. When the change over to rain eventually happened for the Juneau area, larger impacts were seen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry snow began to add up quickly on Sunday the 21st with the NWS office in the Mendenhall Valley measuring over 6 inches by 3pm in the afternoon. Snow continued to fall with warning level rates over the next 2 and a half days, becoming wetter with time, and a short period of freezing rain before temperatures warmed above freezing on the morning of the 24th. The storm total at the Juneau NWS office was 30.9 inches, the Juneau airport had 34.5 inches (11 inches broke the daily record on the 22nd and 14.1 inches broke the daily record on the 23rd), and a COOP observer at Lena Point measured 40.9 inches.||The result was the 2nd multi-day event, each with approximately 3 feet of snowfall, in a week and substantial impacts across the Juneau area. Alaska Electric Light and Power reported a downed tree which took out a power line on the Mendenhall Peninsula. Juneau Harbors reported vessels sinking due to snow load followed by a change to rain, culminating in at least 5 boats sunk. A partial collapse of a warehouse on Channel Drive occurred due to snow load, and two residential porches collapsed in the Mendenhall Valley, with one family temporarily trapped in their home. Basin Road and Thane Road were both closed due to high avalanche danger, and two avalanches were observed on January 24 down the Behrend's Ave path and Basin Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm force low pressure system over the North Pacific lifted north and moved into the Gulf of Alaska by the morning of January 29th. There was an associated atmospheric river along the warm side of the cold front that had a significant amount of sub-tropical moisture within it. Along with the the high anomalous moisture values, there was a very warm airmass with the atmospheric river which increased freezing levels across Southeast Alaska to more than 5000 feet. There was a deep snowpack at upper elevations and near sea level, which melted with the heavy rain to increase the amount of runoff into rivers and streams. Another low pressure system developed along the frontal boundary which stalled the front over the region to produce continued prolog heavy rain but also high winds. The combination of high winds and heavy precipitation on snow at high elevations triggered a number of avalanches near Haines.  ||The 2 day precipitation total across the northern half of the Southeast Alaska ranged from 2 to over 4 inches along with significant amount of snowmelt and runoff from the high freezing levels and the heavy rain melting the snowpack. The heavy rain triggered a small landslide in Haines near Lutak, another near mile 6 of Zimovia Highway in Wrangell. There was some minor overland flooding from poor drainage near the Haines airport by the afternoon of January 29th. Near the Juneau area there was a lot of minor flooding of small streams going over their banks from the rain and snow runoff through January 30th but there was moderate flooding near Jordan Creek that impacted a few homes, business and roadways.||Tree fall was was widespread across the region from this event due to the saturated ground and high wind gusts of 60 to 90 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hydaburg AWOS at the seaplane dock measured periodic high wind gusts through the night of the 28th and into the early morning hours of the 29th. The peak wind gust measured was 86 mph at 907pm on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two Bering Sea low pressure systems tracked into Western Alaska from August 15 through 18th bringing a period of nearly-persistent southerly winds which led to storm surge and coastal flooding along the Kuskokwim Delta coast and backed up flow along the lower Kuskokwim River. Abundant moisture drawn from the tropical West Pacific by both lows squeezed out moderate to heavy rain in Southwest Alaska, which exacerbated flooding issues along the Kuskokwim River. Flooding was most severe on August 18 as the second low began to enter the Bering Strait, bringing the strongest push of south to southwesterly onshore winds. Coastal communities experienced high water levels which inundated boardwalks, flooded subsistence structures, toppled fuel tanks, damaged ATVs and snowmobiles, and affected the foundations of homes. Along the Kuskokwim River, roads were inundated and closed and at least two houses were flooded in the community of Tuntutuliak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between August 15 and 18, water levels exceeded one foot over the normal highest water line during each high tide cycle in both Kwigillingok and Kipnuk. The highest water levels were recorded by tide gauges in both of these communities on the 18th, reaching 2.6 ft above mean higher high water (MHHW) in Kwigillingok and 3.5 ft above MHHW in Kipnuk. A local measuring stake reached around 4 ft in Kipnuk. ||In Kwigillingok, social media and FAA webcam imagery show water levels reached up to the boardwalk, moving boats and other property left on normally dry, low-lying areas. A large fuel tank was displaced. In Kipnuk, a local official reported that water entered the first floor of a house along the slough, a wooden shack along the riverbank was washed away, a 500 gallon fuel storage tank floated away, and portions of the public boardwalk system were damaged. An unsecured heating oil tank also spilled, releasing less than 55 gallons. Increased rates of erosion were observed as a result of the storm, which damaged the barge landing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers developing near a weak low moving over the Gulf of Alaska fueled by seasonably warm ocean surface temperatures coupled with cool air aloft near the low produced at least one waterspout documented by a mariner south of Resurrection Bay.  The shower that produced the waterspout was fairly shallow and weak based on satellite and radar imagery, and rotation associated with the vortex was likely on far too small of a scale to be detected by remote sensing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mariner reported that a waterspout developed between Cheval Island and Pony Cove starting around 10:45 AM AKST that moved from west to east after developing and lasted for about 5 minutes.  Photo evidence provided by this mariner showed a clear funnel cloud and surface circulation kicking up sea spray.  While this waterspout developed close to several islands, there was no clear evidence this feature ever reached land before it dissipated.

Snowfall rates increased across the central panhandle Sunday morning, causing the onset time of the event to begin earlier and resulting in a longer duration snowfall, but warning level rates were confined to the Icy Strait region with greatest amounts in the Juneau area. Dry snow became wetter through the event with southern areas transitioning to rain before seeing significant accumulations. When the change over to rain eventually happened for the Juneau area, larger impacts were seen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Gustavus saw continuous snowfall over the 3 day event and gave regular updates of snow accumulations, including periods of warning level rates (3 inches in 6 hours) during the evening of the 21st, during the daytime on the 22nd, and the afternoon of the 23rd. Her storm total was 22 inches due to compaction. A nearby COOP observer measured a storm total of 26.7 inches of snow from a multi-day snowfall event. The snowfall was heaviest on the 23rd, resulting in a 24 hour measurement of 11.3 inches by the following morning at 5am.

The winds reached a maximum speed of 62 mph at 1:05 and 4:25 AM at the Metlakatla AWOS on the morning of January 29th and a similar gust was measured at the Weather Service Station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong high pressure over the Arctic Ocean combined with an area of low pressure in the Bering Sea brought strong winds to the Arctic Coast resulting in blizzard conditions developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Point Lay the morning of the first and continued through the morning of the second. Point Lay recorded a peak wind gust of 52 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong high pressure over the Arctic Ocean combined with an area of low pressure in the Bering Sea brought strong winds to the Arctic Coast resulting in blizzard conditions developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard condition developed the the morning of the second and continued through the morning of the 5th. Multiple sites reported blizzard conditions, including Alpine, Deadhorse, Kuparuk and Nuiqsut. Wind gusts as high as 58 mph were observed at Deadhorse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of strong high pressure over the Arctic Ocean combined with an area of low pressure in the Bering Sea brought strong winds to the Arctic Coast resulting in blizzard conditions developing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed early in the morning of the third and continued through the morning of the 5th. Point Thomson reported winds as high as 40 mph during this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Cape Romonzof the morning of the 6th and continued through the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front associated with a strong area of low pressure moving north through the Bering Sea brought blizzard conditions to the Yukon Delta and High Winds to the Seward Peninsula.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at Nome increased the night of the 6th and during the day of the 7th of January. A peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded the afternoon of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of very strong high pressure over Interior Alaska and an area of low pressure over the Beaufort Sea brought strong winds and blowing snow to the Beaufort Sea Coast of Alaska on February 3, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed the morning of the 3rd and continued through that evening. Blowing snow reduced the visibility for most of the day, with blizzard conditions being observed from late morning into the early evening. A peak wind gust of 40 mph was recorded at Point Thomson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moving to the north over the Alaska Range brought high winds near passes along the north side of the range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds developed through Windy Pass late in the evening on the 12th and continued into the early evening of the 13th. The DOT Antler Creek mesonet station reported a peak gust of 81 mph during the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions developed at Point Lay the morning of the 14th and continued through early afternoon. Winds as high as 50 mph also occurred during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of high pressure over the southern Chukchi Sea combined with an area of low pressure in the southern Bering Sea brought strong winds and blizzard conditions to the Bering Strait on February 16th.

A trained spotter at Robertson River reported estimated wind gusts of 50 mph on the evening of the 20th. The trained spotter also reported a few trees were knocked over by the strong winds on his property.
